summary: I was today years old when i found out about a tragedy. I fully started listening to Her's just recently, wonderful lads,...
text: I was today years old when i found out about a tragedy. I fully started listening to Her's just recently, wonderful lads,... I was today years old when i found out about a tragedy. I fully started listening to Her's just recently, wonderful lads, extremely talented to the core..which i found out went missing with their manager 7 years ago, due to a car crash. today it's been 1 month since the 7th anniversary of their disappearance, and i was litteraly listening to Speed racer when i found out about this, i'm completely destroyed. When now i have their songs in my head, i think about how their life was taken from them in just one moment, they were two super sweet guys of just 24 and 25 years old, 24 AND 25 YEARS OLD, and their manager WAS JUST 37, it's cruel how nature can just bring away such young souls, as i read about them, they deeply cared about us fans, and surely they were so full of energy and wonderful ideas, there was something magic in their music that can't be really described, they've been one of my biggest inspirations recently, i was trembling, felt i was about to cry, this makes us learn how we must hold on to our lives, we must live and enjoy the smallest moments, cuz we won't ever know what is gonna happen to us, and this is scary. With said, rest in peace guys, you'll be surely playing something in the clouds now💜🙇🏼🙇🏼 I was today years old when i found out about a tragedy. tags: noah kahan|the great divide|the last of the bugs|music review|indie music|music|new music|musician|music lovers|album|indie|lea's yapping corner|indie rock|album of the week|folk music|indie folk|music industry|music releases|music recs|music blog|music ramblings|music related
summary: Album Of The Week💿
text: Album Of The Week💿 Album Of The Week💿 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ One Album per Week for a whole year ―୨୧⋆ ˚ 14/52 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ rating: 4/10 -> The Great Divide: The Last Of The Bugs by Noah Kahan I think a 'Sophomore Album Curse' can also apply to the album that comes after your break-through album, one of them being 'The Great Divide: The Last Of The Bugs'. What seems like a good sound for one or two albums to get started, can become repetitive when you are working on your fourth studio album. In true reflection, I have not listened to more Noah Kahan than his third studio album 'Stick Season' from 2022 and a few singles like 'Mess' or 'Maine' and I get that folk is genre and style and I'm not saying anything against staying in your genre of preference but where does comfortability end and repetitiveness start? While some songs sound more country inspired, e.g. 'Headed North' with its acoustic stripped back slightly out of tune guitar, raw vocals and 4-beat kick-drum, a lot of the album sounds like another deluxe version of 'Stick Season'. Only to release a deluxe album including 4 new songs a day after the original release. It's like reheating old nachos that still taste good but don't hold the same spice as they did when you first opened the package. I am not saying, that an artist needs to change with every album and turn their back on what they build previously, but what am I supposed to do when I can't differentiate between 2 albums that have 4 years between them? Whether that be in topic or sound. 90 minutes don't feel like 90 minutes when your brain already sees everything as familiar and tunes out most of the time that is spent telling stories that it already knows by heart. I don't want Noah Kahan to change, I just want him to try something new for once. Take on a darker tone or heavier themes and blend them with his indie-folk sound. Deliver themes of grief and anxiety in a way that feels threatening and anxious. Use his voice more, let himself scream and yearn and cry out for what he wants instead of keeping his emotions at bay in favor of not scaring away the main-stream tiktok listeners. text: Solfeggio Dream Solfeggio Dream Find A Song about dissociation, introspection, and the cyclical nature of consciousnes s Last Relapse - Solfeggio Dream This guitars-and-vocals-only composition opens with a mesmerizing 9/8 time signature complemented by reverse delay guitar effects, immediately establishing its dreamlike atmosphere before ambient vocals drift into the mix. Tuned to the Solfeggio frequency—ancient tones believed to possess healing properties—the track creates what Holding describes as "a floating euphoric dream." The accompanying lyrics explore themes of dissociation, introspection, and the cyclical nature of consciousness, painting vivid imagery of mental landscapes and the multiplicity of self. I grew up on old Black Sabbath and continue to be inspired by those heavy guitars propelled by drums that swing like mad. This track was recorded using my P-90 equipped Gibson SG, into a Roger Mayer Axis fuzz I picked up in the early 90's, which is in an alternate tuning and layered multiple times, going through my beloved Marshall JCM800 I bought in '82. summary: Spotify Followers vs Monthly Listeners: Which One Actually Matters for Your Career
text: Spotify Followers vs Monthly Listeners: Which One Actually Matters for Your Career monthly listeners = what happened in the last 28 days. followers = how many people will hear your next release on day one. one is a temperature check. the other is distribution infrastructure. an artist with 3,000 monthly listeners and 2,500 followers is in a stronger position than one with 15,000 monthly listeners and 800 followers. the algorithm calculates ratios, not totals. stop chasing the number that spikes and crashes. start building the one that compounds. full breakdown: Spotify Followers vs Monthly Listeners: Which One Actually Matters for Your Career monthly listeners = what happened in the last 28 days. followers = how many people will hear your next release on day one. one is a temperature check. the other is distribution infrastructure. an artist with 3,000 monthly listeners and 2,500 followers is in a stronger position than one with 15,000 monthly listeners and 800 followers. the algorithm calculates ratios, not totals. stop chasing the number that spikes and crashes. start building the one that compounds. full breakdown: Spotify Followers vs Monthly Listeners: Which One Actually Matters for Your Career

summary: Why Spotify Removed Your Followers (And What It Means for Your Account)
text: Why Spotify Removed Your Followers (And What It Means for Your Account) your spotify follower count dropped overnight and nobody told you why. here's what happened: spotify runs batch purges that wipe bot accounts. every artist connected to those accounts loses followers instantly. the visible drop isn't the worst part. the real damage happened while those bots were inflating your count. your engagement ratio tanked and the algorithm started treating you like nobody cared about your releases. but here's the counterintuitive part: a purge can actually help if your remaining followers are real. smaller but cleaner base = better ratios = more algorithmic support. full breakdown: Why Spotify Removed Your Followers (And What It Means for Your Account) your spotify follower count dropped overnight and nobody told you why. here's what happened: spotify runs batch purges that wipe bot accounts. every artist connected to those accounts loses followers instantly. the visible drop isn't the worst part. the real damage happened while those bots were inflating your count. your engagement ratio tanked and the algorithm started treating you like nobody cared about your releases. but here's the counterintuitive part: a purge can actually help if your remaining followers are real. smaller but cleaner base = better ratios = more algorithmic support. full breakdown: Why Spotify Removed Your Followers (And What It Means for Your Account)

summary: Found Footage by PULLSWITCH
text: Found Footage by PULLSWITCH I'm still a liar, don't trust me for a little while... Found Footage (my 3rd EP), comes out on May 8th!!! I've been waiting for a long time to get to say this because I've been working on these songs for a little while now. Found Footage by PULLSWITCH Last time I put out an EP, it was very haphazard becuase I needed to get it out of my system, which lead to a less than favorable finished product on the production and vocal department. For this release, I spent a lot longer crafting the songs and perfecting what I wanted from a sound standpoint. Obviously there is gonna be rough patches, I'm doing this all by myself. But some of my proudest work so far is contained in this project so I'm excited for it. Two of these songs (tracks 2 and 6) were actually taken from an earlier scrapped project I started in September to October of last year and reworked for this and the first song after that that I started writing for this was in February to put in perspective how long this has taken to finish. A lot of the writing here is my most personal stuff yet, so I was hesitant to share, but I needed to get it out. And a lot of these things I have moved on from since, which made it easier to share. On top of that, it's one of my most varied releases, especially in length (shortest song is 2 ½ minutes, longest is 8 ½), so I was ready to share my new genreadventures. As I mentioned above, 2 of the songs featured are from a scrapped project, which featured 2 other songs. As I do with every release, there will be a second "duo" EP that releases which will contain instrumentals for every song on the EP, but this time also demos for some songs, including ones from that scrapped project and another song that didn't make it on to this EP, and which probably won't find a home in a different one. That second EP is called Found Footage (Director's Bonus), because I wanted to do a funny movie reference since the title of the EP is a reference to a film subgenre/technique. See you at the next project Tracklist: 1. That's who I have to be (8:33) 2. PAPER CRANE (4:18) 3. Naegleria Fowleri (3:30) 4. in shadows (3:15) 5. Poison Season (3:09) 6. summary: When no real harm remains and no literary death condemns another soul to the scarlet mark of poetry, I shall remain faithful to...
text: When no real harm remains and no literary death condemns another soul to the scarlet mark of poetry, I shall remain faithful to... When no real harm remains and no literary death condemns another soul to the scarlet mark of poetry, I shall remain faithful to my own darkness. To my quiet refusals, to the elegant persistence of my lies. Among the wreckage of heaven’s quiet collapse I still endure, haunted by the sickly memory of what it once meant to love you. So many wars crossed. So many duels with restless spirits offended by the mere sound of truth. Or perhaps it was only my stubborn need to uncover what never ceased to be shadow. Your gaze no longer tells stories. And I might weep for such a melancholy ending — for having remained so childishly faithful to someone like you, who never existed beyond the fragile province of dream. Believe me: passion is no balm. And yet, had my eyes not burned in the quiet fire of your presence — in the slow exhaustion of loving you — nothing in this world could have unsettled my heart again… unless, perhaps, another judgement is already waiting for me in the dark. summary: Can U?
text: Can U? Can U? wearing my lipstick, cherry red and loud smile through the breakdowns, pretty for crowd lately , i feel like i've been living inside by this song. like putting on my " cherry red lipstick " version of myself every day, trying to look fine , trying to act like everything is under control , even when it's really not. i laugh, i talk , i show up ... but inside, it's messy , unfinished and still hurting in ways i don't fully understand. there's this strange comfort in not wanting to be fixed. i don't want someone to come in and clean everything up or tell me how to be okay . it's more like… i just want to feel it fully, even if it burns a little. even if i ruin some things along the way. because at least then it's honest . at least then it’s real . some days i feel like i'm spinning out, like i might crash at any moment, but a part of me doesn't even want to stop it. i just want someone to stay , to not reach for the wheel, to not try to control me, but to understand me. to see me exactly like this, unpolished and a little broken , and still choose to stay. i think that's what's been sitting with me the most lately . the question of whether someone could love me like this. not the version that looks okay , but the one that feels alone even in a crowded room. the one that carries loud memories and quiet breakdowns . and maybe that's why this song feels so close to me right now. it sounds like everything i haven't been able to say out loud. summary: Found out my mom is in the hospital with an ulcer - send good thoughts her way - coincidentally I made this album months ago but...
text: Found out my mom is in the hospital with an ulcer - send good thoughts her way - coincidentally I made this album months ago but... Found out my mom is in the hospital with an ulcer - send good thoughts her way - coincidentally I made this album months ago but it just released today and it is all about sickness In family. Weird no? Weirder is how I write my music. I call it subconscious writing I dunno I just turn on autocomplete and let my eyes glaze over and or look away multitask watching tv whatever and ask ChatGPT to scan it for not literal interpretation but underlying repetitive themes and symbols and then work with that as a basis for my lyrics. I just watched a program a day or two ago that said people with epilepsy were more likely to have psychic abilities. I dunno if I believe in that all but can’t deny this is freaky coincidence. My grandma kept calling and Wednesday is my busy back to back meeting day. But one of the lines in the song says “Mom called sounded scared to death. My telephone won’t rest” well it was nana calling crying gasping and saying she was dying not literally but from stress but it was my mom who was sick and i was in meetings so i had to go and tell her i would call after work which i did but another line in the song says “I’m pulled in a hundred ways tonight, trying to hold on to the light. I’m in the storm and back under it again” There is another track different song same album again about a mom being sick. Then there is one about despite everything feeling grateful and sending love to everyone. Cheer me up - give me some streams, likes and shares, and send positive thoughts to my entire family- mom for her sickness, nana for her stress, and my sadness for both of them. https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=OLAK5uy_mtNwrwS9YRnzd_YEcXNhfuv57XAjbabgo&si=zIbTruDl4rWidIKM Found out my mom is in the hospital with an ulcer - send good thoughts her way - coincidentally I made this album months ago but it just released today and it is all about sickness In family. summary: Song of the Period of Time Until My Next Post
text: Song of the Period of Time Until My Next Post Song of the Period of Time Until My Next Post Hi :) So! Riverside had a nice long run because work has been my entire life for several months but I’m back in action for at least long enough to write this. Whisky Tide - Ferester Run Time: 3:24 Genre: Indie, folk, kinda country-ish vibe in parts? “If I were the waves, and you were the coast, I’d crash in your bays and drift to your coves // “Wishing I could stay forever on your shores.” I first heard this song in the background of one of my friend’s Snapchat stories. This is easily one of my favorite love songs I know. There’s no grand declaration or promises nobody’s going to keep. Time goes on, and on and on, but no matter what happens or where two people end up, the love, the ache is still there. And maybe one day it will be different, and they’ll have all the time in the world. Until then, what they have is enough. My favorite parts of this song include, of course, the coastal imagery, as someone from Maine who never shuts the fuck up about being from Maine (did you guys know I’m from Maine btw?) and the bridge toward the end because overlapping vocals 😍 It is late April which means I am in the lull between my two seasonal jobs. This is typically when I latch onto a love song and start believing I’ll actually relate to it one day. A direct result of 1) starting to feel ever so slightly human again with time off and 2) knowing it will be starting to warm up into the 50°-60°s consistently pretty soon. I will get over myself soon, but in the meantime, listen to Whisky Tide! If you end up liking this song, I also recommend: Ferester’s other music!!! Back to Us - Ike Dweck Badlands - Harrison Boe Get Older - Harrison Boe Anything by Harrison Boe I’m going to make a separate Harrison Boe appreciation post. text: Whiplash Whiplash Find A Song about not being able to let go of the things that haunt you Annabelle Tiffin - Whiplash Whiplash is about not being able to let go of the things that still haunt you, even long after they’ve happened. The initial shock might be gone, but the feeling never fully leaves. It just sits there, bitter and heavy. text: From Taare Zameen Par to Sitaare Zameen Par: Aamir Khan’s Impact on Art & Learning in India From Taare Zameen Par to Sitaare Zameen Par: Aamir Khan’s Impact on Art & Learning in India Let’s rewind to 2007 for a second. You’re sitting in a theatre, watching Taare Zameen Par , and suddenly — it hits you. That scene where Ishaan breaks down during his exam? Yeah, that one stayed with all of us. Because for the first time, we realized something uncomfortable: Maybe the problem wasn’t the child. Maybe it was the system. Fast forward to today, and Aamir Khan is back with Sitaare Zameen Par on SonyLIV — and honestly, the message feels even more relevant now. Why That Film Still Matters Before Taare Zameen Par , most people in India didn’t even know what dyslexia was. After it? Parents started noticing. Teachers started caring. Conversations started happening. That’s the power of storytelling done right. Art Was Never “Just a Period” Remember how art class used to be treated? Free period. Timepass. Something “extra.” But Nikumbh flipped that idea completely. He showed us that art is not about drawing neatly — it’s about expressing what words cannot. What’s New in 2025? Sitaare Zameen Par takes things further. It talks about neurodivergent adults, inclusion, and respect — not sympathy. And honestly, that shift matters. A lot. Big Shift in Thinking Earlier: “My child isn’t talented” “Focus on studies only” Now: “My child has a different strength” “Creativity matters too” And Here’s the Best Part In 2007, you needed a great teacher to unlock potential. In 2026? You just need internet. Online art classes, dance sessions, creative workshops — everything is accessible now. Bottom Line These films aren’t just movies. They’re reminders. summary: Gabrielle Moura on Instagram: "Another sleepless night. Writing. Thinking. Writing. You walk the streets far from me, with...
text: Gabrielle Moura on Instagram: "Another sleepless night. Writing. Thinking. Writing. You walk the streets far from me, with... Another sleepless night. Writing. Thinking. Writing. You walk the streets far from me, with someone else — the one who occupies the space that was never mine. I wish I could interfere with your thoughts, watch, just once, the thing I desire and that is always denied me come into being. It is written on my forehead. I wish I could alter your biology. Now. Interrupt this useless cult — so many candles for a profane god who never descends. My hand to my face, rehearsing impossible futures, imagining how it would be if you were someone else. To share an energy — anything that might save me from the imminence of madness. But as it began, it ended inside my head. Collapsed onto paper. This coarse heart, bleeding for someone wealthy enough to ignore it. Hands in pockets, begging for validation. The miracle everyone promises — and that never comes. text: This is pretty late for being my 1st gig of the year but nothing has tempted me out so far until this one. Valentine's day as... This is pretty late for being my 1st gig of the year but nothing has tempted me out so far until this one. Valentine's day as well, so what else do you do when you're single? I thought the guy in @paintedvein_ was familiar and I knew that he'd collaborated with @moaamusic on one of his songs, but didn't realise until I spoke to him that he was the other part of MØAA. summary: "Virgin" by Lorde
text: "Virgin" by Lorde "Virgin" by Lorde The first record I want to talk about is one I had sitting in my "pre-save" section for a long time. When Virgin was first released, I didn't quite connect with it. To be honest, after the masterpiece that was Melodrama (which is easily in my top 10 favorite albums of all time). I had certain expectations that Solar Power hadn't met, and I wasn't sure where Virgin would fit but it really surprised me. That changed at the end of 2025. I was heading home for the holidays, feeling spiritless and deeply sad. It was a difficult Christmas, but with what little strength I had left, I got out of bed and caught my bus. I put my headphones on, scrolled through my library, and there it was: Virgin . At the time, I was navigating the end of several friendships, rethinking my relationship, and mourning my college years as I faced the pressure of "adulting." I felt like nobody understood my grief. For my peers, the transition seemed natural: college ends, you get a job. But for me, it was different. I was just beginning to realize who I wanted to be as an artist, and I felt like I was the only one without a map. Everything felt like it was falling apart, but Lorde’s perspective in Virgin made me realize: "Wow, she actually gets it." She made me feel like the end of the world wasn't actually the end. It’s okay not to have everything figured out. This was the record that helped me close the door on a heavy 2025 and step into 2026 with more confidence. Less scared of not knowing everything, to not have an answer and with hope to meet this new me and whatever life would bring to me. And I loved how she made my walk of shame back home from being a failure in the city to an okay let's dance and sink in the feeling of the growing pains and melancholy. She let me feel all I have to feel to finally set free from my fears. Cause with this record i allow myself through his rhythm all the sadness, all the angst, the rage, the confusion. Everything. Here's some lyrics from the record that put everything in perspective to me: Hammer: “I might have been born again I'm ready to feel like I don't have thе answers There's pеace in the madness over our heads Let it carry me u-u-u-u-up” “Let it break me down till I'm just a breath Till I'm just a voice living in your head It's a fucked-up world, been to hell and back But I've sent you a postcard from the edge” What Was That? “You had to know this was happening You weren't feeling my heat When I'm in the blue light, down at Baby's All Right I face reality” “I try (I try), to let (To let) Whatever has to pass through me pass through But this is staying a while, I know It might not let me go” Have you ever had an album you ignored suddenly become the only thing that understood you? Find A Song that is a lament for London’s East End Eugene McGuinness - Eastend Requiem ‘Eastend Requiem’ is a soothing introspective gem that sparkles and sashays with a pop melancholy and a nod to bands like The Divine Comedy and Tindersticks. Eugene’s own trademark lyrical wit and flair is woven between glistening synths, syncopated percussion and bittersweet Parisian orchestral flourishes, as the track grows into a stunning, harmony-laden lament for London’s East End, which is both candid and gritty, yet beautiful in its reflection. summary: The prince, enthroned above his audience, regards the subjects to whom he once declaimed that all would be welcome, that there...
text: The prince, enthroned above his audience, regards the subjects to whom he once declaimed that all would be welcome, that there... The prince, enthroned above his audience, regards the subjects to whom he once declaimed that all would be welcome, that there would always be room at the table. How exquisitely generous. The error came when he noticed that the halls — once gilded, once fertile — formerly inhabited by his vigorous lovers (entirely imaginary, but no less demanding), were now filled with people who arrived without asking permission and, worse, stayed. Forgive him. He was young. He mistook beauty for immunity, like a rose cultivated to believe it bloomed by merit alone, feeding elegantly on rejection, calling solitude a virtue so long as it was admired. There was, once, a dim room where a select few were invited to feel chosen. Now the charming host reprimands his guests for breathing incorrectly, for laughing at the wrong moment, for existing without rehearsal. He repeats the same gestures, calls rigidity courage, calls fear conviction, and insists that cruelty, when delivered politely, is merely honesty with good posture. Is it not touching how often weakness disguises itself as principle? Melancholy, once hospitable, has become a fortified inheritance — barbed wire, sacred geese, and a past that patrols itself. Nothing consoles. Nothing welcomes. Be loyal. Lower your eyes. Kneel if necessary. This is how one honours a king who once swore he despised crowns and now cannot sleep without one. There is something irresistibly comic about the prince who loathed authority becoming the sovereign of prohibitions, dreaming of obedience while posing as a martyr — as though the crown were thorns and not precisely tailored gold. Do not look up. Do not smile. Do not notice that truth, like laughter, is heavier than he expected. He confuses being unloved with being profound. He mistakes offence for sincerity, and solitude for indispensability. A common tragedy, performed with exceptional diction. The devoted lover, once ancient and sincere, escaped quietly through the window, tired of being summoned, accused, or punished for requesting air. What remains is the pose. The gesture. The beautifully rehearsed complaint. An emissary will read, in the public square, a letter addressed to the ungrateful — allegedly written by history, sounding remarkably like an old man furious that applause no longer arrives on cue. And so it ends, as it must, with the finest irony of all: to mistake oblivion for persecution, and call it company. (Gabrielle Moura) https://www.instagram.com/p/DW9WIMpDqqk/?igsh=MjF1cXB0ajl1bjVy The prince, enthroned above his audience, regards the subjects to whom he once declaimed that all would be welcome, that there would always be room at the table. Couldn’t register my car because insurance and NV has very dumb laws. I came home and finished mixing this track and couldn’t even fully be happy about it. I stress about money. A lot. I grew up broke and use it as a hunger to keep working my day job and taking rap seriously but what happens when your hungry and need to use your energy for something? 🤷🏾‍♂️ Exactly. Plus my sinuses are draining…joy. First track with a major producer and I’m not even sure the mix is good enough to drop because I can’t hear 100%.. a friend of mine sent me some money to cover my DMV fees for at least one of my cars. So the wife and I can at least car pool until my check hits..fuck this shit sucks sometimes. I hate asking for help. Few people actually came to help in the past so having people do stuff for me is hard..I am flying to Oakland on my vacation to pay her back. No doubt. I have to. text: Spotify Growth for US Artists: What Actually Works in the American Market the us is spotify's biggest and most brutal market. 100,000+ tracks uploaded daily. major label budgets on every playlist. and most independent artists are stuck under 1,000 followers wondering why nobody hears them. here's what's actually working: build a concentrated listener base in your city first. portland, atlanta, nashville, wherever. own that market. the algorithm picks up the geographic cluster and starts expanding you outward to similar listeners in nearby cities. and your follower count controls your release radar reach. more real followers = more first-week impressions = stronger algorithmic signal. that's not theory. that's how the infrastructure works. full strategy breakdown: Spotify Growth for US Artists: What Actually Works in the American Market the us is spotify's biggest and most brutal market. 100,000+ tracks uploaded daily. major label budgets on every playlist. and most independent artists are stuck under 1,000 followers wondering why nobody hears them. here's what's actually working: build a concentrated listener base in your city first. portland, atlanta, nashville, wherever. own that market. the algorithm picks up the geographic cluster and starts expanding you outward to similar listeners in nearby cities. and your follower count controls your release radar reach. more real followers = more first-week impressions = stronger algorithmic signal. that's not theory. that's how the infrastructure works. full strategy breakdown: Spotify Growth for US Artists: What Actually Works in the American Market

Evil Factory Breakroom PinPigeon Forest's Last Valiant Soldier PinPigeon Forest's Last Valiant Soldier PinPigeon Here's some more music while I'm trying to figure out the new video, :) Both of these originally started with just some 3/4 practice that I turned into loops so I could use them in my games in future. I think they do a good-ish job at setting an atmosphere, at the least. Evil Factory Breakroom sounds like you're in an evil factory, Forest's Last Valiant Soldier sounds like the last battle of a forest, and it losing. I don't know what I'm actually going to use these for in future, but they're useful. If ever I need to skimp out on music in a game jam again, at least I'll have better options for last-minute music. tags: music|indie music|music lovers|shame|shame band|cutthroat|indie|new music|album|lea's yapping corner|indie rock|album of the week|music industry|musician|album cover|new album|album art|indie bands|post punk music|post punk
summary: Album Of The Week💿
text: Album Of The Week💿 Album Of The Week💿 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ One Album per Week for a whole year ―୨୧⋆ ˚ 12/52 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ rating: 7.65/10 -> Cutthroat by Shame I'm not too sure which part of my mind expected this album to be more punk-leaning after seeing half of an interview of this band without any further context before going into it. And besides a few punk-inspired edges and a lot of curse words, the album is far away from being a disappointment even if it's not what I thought it would be. Shame, a band that, after watching a few live performances on social media, seems like the picture perfect post punk band brings their raw lyrics and energetic rhythms to the dance floor of an half burned down club. The disco ball is barely holding on but still reflecting the flickering lights on raw, broken pieces. 'Cutthroat' is the first album on my journey into discovering a new artist within a genre I grew comfortable and familiar in over the years and while everyone might see it a bit different, this album isn't primarily one I would've put into the category of 'post-punk' without hesitation. Music is a wide spectrum, it reaches from people not seeing The Doors as a hippie band (me) to people (Inhaler) saying that Nirvana's 'Nevermind' can be categorized as an pop album—which in the traditional sense of pop isn't wrong, since it was popular and still is, though today that genre has it's own particular sound. And that's only a few examples of people not agreeing over music labels, there are about a hundred more circulating just in this second. Even with it's genre shifting sound, jumping between post-punk and pop-/indie-rock depending whether the chorus or verse is on, the album holds weight to it's name. Though those shifting moments are also the ones that stick. Take the chorus from the title track, 'Cutthroat'. Transferring the repetition of 'I was born to die' into a smooth, almost compressed sound of calling out for freedom, a moment of closing your eyes within the chaos of sweating in the pit before jumping right back into the mosh-pit gives the song what it needs to stay interesting. Instead of an endless loop of limping drums and threshing guitars, they implement an almost vision shifting feeling of transformation between the instruments and Charlie Steen's honest lyrics of what the world looks like from his perspective. On 'Lampião' the five-piece tells the story of a Brazillian bandit leader in the 1920s. Blending playful, almost ironic sounding Portuguese backing vocals that depict the situation before Lampião murder and the string of a narrator from the future, talking to and about the dead man. Not everything that can be defined, should be defined, that's what 'Shame' emphasize from the very beginning of their fourth record and it's exactly what describes this record best. After over ten years in the industry, they know that not being too serious in their music makes them all the more serious as musicians for the long run. summary: Intro to Spotstuk:
text: Intro to Spotstuk: Intro to Spotstuk: Spotstuk is a farming based game where you can build your farm but you also have to fight monsters away from your farm and close the portal they are coming in from. You also have options to go into town, sell your crops, develop friendships, and solve puzzles. There are multiple paths you can go down, choosing to be a mage will strengthen your ability to fight and choosing scribe will give you the ability to access more techniques in battle. This is an indie game project that me and my friend, Wh1teC0coa started. However, it’s really just a concept on the drawing board right now. There isn’t much done. We would like your help with this! If you are someone who can contribute to the game please consider applying! Animation: The style of this game is in pixel art so if you believe that you are suited to be an animator in this style let us know! I will be 100% willing to send you samples of our style! Character design: We are looking for help making our characters more interesting, we are also looking to make them distinguishable in pixel art. Programing: As mentioned earlier neither of the people running the project right now know anything about programing or coding. We are looking for someone who can. Music artists: We are searching for people who can make indie electronic music for fights, backgrounds music, and overall themes. Storyline writer: Indie games are famously known for their Easter eggs, complex storylines, and small details that matter. We would like help on this aspect!! summary: Pixies' Black Francis: "I'd put a record out every six months if they'd let me"
text: Pixies' Black Francis: "I'd put a record out every six months if they'd let me" “The record is just proof you’re still alive… that you’ve still got some brain cells left.” That’s where Black Francis started when we got into the Pixies album The Night the Zombies Came and what it even means to release music now. He was saying streaming flipped the whole thing. People weren’t buying records the same way, but they were still paying for shows, sometimes more than ever. Which meant new albums weren’t about hits anymore, they were about staying in the conversation. “It’s validation,” he said. You put something out, people know you’re still doing it. And then he got into how that actually freed him up. Less pressure from labels, less obsession over numbers, more just making records because he wanted to. He said he’d put one out every six months if he could. #Pixies #NewMusic Pixies' Black Francis: "I'd put a record out every six months if they'd let me" “The record is just proof you’re still alive… that you’ve still got some brain cells left.” That’s where Black Francis started when we got into the Pixies album The Night the Zombies Came and what it even means to release music now. tags: wet leg|music|moisturizer|music lovers|indie music|album of the week|album|new music|indie|indie rock|lea's yapping corner|alternative rock|alternative|album art|new album|grammys|british music|brit awards
summary: Album Of The Week💿
text: Album Of The Week💿 Album Of The Week💿 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ One Album per Week for a whole year ―୨୧⋆ ˚ 11/52 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ rating: 8/10 -> moisturizer by Wet Leg The indie rock artists are in love. Whether you take Fontaines D.C.'s latest album 'Romance' or Olivia Rodrigo's upcoming project—rumoured to be titled 'love'—the genre is being filled with one specific topic: being in love and loving. The same can be taken from the British duo 'Wet Leg' on their latest album 'moisturizer'. Embracing emotions like sexual desire as on 'pillow talk' or insecurities in a relationship as on 'liquidize', the duo doesn't let societal structures that still stand within the music industry keep them from writing music. Creating an alternative rock album that makes you want to be in the band not just listen to it. After being nominated at this year's Grammys for 'Best Album Cover' as well as at the Brits for 'Best Alternative/Rock Act' and 'Group Of The Year', Wet Leg are still on the road up until July of 2026 and they don't seem to be able to stop. Taking over social media with their track 'mangetout' earlier this year and stepping on red carpets with their own creative decisions. Often being quoted as a 'spark of hope' when it comes to the alternative rock genre Wet Leg are surely one to keep an eye on. summary: Poison Season by PULLSWITCH
text: Poison Season by PULLSWITCH Could I just take a break for a little while? 'Poison Season', my new song, comes out APRIL 10TH!!! I'm elated to put it out because it's one of my favorites I've ever made and it's one of my few rock songs so far (more to come 😛). This took longer than usual because I've been trying to perfect what I'm going for. I put out my last EP a little haphazardly and it shows in a lot of my vocals, so I tried to take my time with this upcoming batch of songs. It'll be a little longer until the EP drops but I think it'll be worth it for myself. If I haven't mentioned before, I make music that I listen to, but I decided to start sharing it so on the off chance other people also enjoy what I write, they can get something out of it as well. Now, since my 2nd EP has been out for a bit and I haven't found myself listening to it as much as my first, I understand that I need to make some improvements. I think Poison Season was the first step to that. Though it only took me about 3 hours to write the music and lyrics for it, it took me many more days to record vox and make sure production sufficed. I'm also working on a music video for it so expect that soon. Just need some to do some rain shots tomorrow... The new EP: It's longer than my last two. (~30 minutes compared to 24 and 15). And it has 7 songs, compared to 6 and 5. One of the songs is 8 ½ minutes long and another is 2 ½, so I'm really going for variety on this one. It also has some of my most personal writing ever. While I've written about my life many times before, I've never been this direct, so I'm a little hesitant to share, but I think it's for the best. 2 of the songs on the project were ones I wrote in September and October for an EP that is now scrapped, but I liked the composition of both of them enough to carry them over with revamped instrumentals that fit the vibe that was originally intended. One of them also got a lyrical overhaul because I didn't like the original version. The demos for the songs meant for that scrapped EP as well as the original versions of those two songs will be included on the Demos & Instrumentals EP that I always put out with an EP release. Until then, may you suffer Poison Season by PULLSWITCH Could I just take a break for a little while? 'Poison Season', my new song, comes out APRIL 10TH!!! summary: Ironclad and Palm trees
text: Ironclad and Palm trees Ironclad and Palm trees Find A Song about the sacrifices related to migration Andervel - Ironclad and Palm trees “Looking at a palm tree through the window of her room, that’s how my grandmother spent a couple of hours each day in the twilight of her life. In that tree, a small flock of colourful budgies had nested. My grandparents lost a daughter in 1994, and she used to say that when she died, she would come back as a budgie to visit her mother. This song was born during a period of anticipatory grief as my grandmother’s health slowly declined, culminating in her passing. One of the hardest sacrifices of migration is building a life far from the people you first love, shaping an identity that exists between two worlds. The song was written and recorded in Reykjavík, with half of my thoughts in Mexico with my grandma in spirit, while I walked among the ironclad houses on my way to the studio downtown. The lyrics draw on memories of my grandmother’s home: the palm tree across the street, the budgies nesting in its crown, ivy covering the front gate, and spiders weaving webs between the walls each spring. I spent much of my childhood there, and my grandparents were a fundamental part of my upbringing.” Added to FAS Spotify playlist indie folk/country/americana. summary: Many live albums are problematic for countless reasons. This one is not. Here, the energy of the performance does not rely...
text: Many live albums are problematic for countless reasons. This one is not. Here, the energy of the performance does not rely... Many live albums are problematic for countless reasons. This one is not. Here, the energy of the performance does not rely solely on the past: the music, the lyrics, the atmosphere… everything contributes to tracing Marr’s trajectory as an artist. There is a balance between solo material, moments from Electronic, a few covers, and the classics from The Smiths — songs that have defined generations, without ever sounding like a concession. There is strength, precision, and, above all, identity. Marr does not revisit — he reaffirms. And that is dangerous… and absolutely necessary when it is not about self-indulgence, but rather an honest re-reading of who he is, without asking for permission. Each guitar line is clean, direct, almost surgical — and it receives an immediate response from the audience, as if recognising something that never truly disappeared. In the end, Look Out Live! is not merely a live record. It is proof that Johnny Marr has never needed to live in anyone’s shadow — not his own, not others’, not even that of his own legacy. He keeps creating, playing, moving forward — with the same restless elegance as ever and that almost unreal air of someone who seems not to belong entirely anywhere — and refuses to turn into a statue. summary: The Laws of Life
text: The Laws of Life The Laws of Life Find A Song about going back in time to give yourself advice Night Wolf , Lois Powell - The Laws of Life “The Laws Of Life” is the fourth collaboration from Lois Powell and Night Wolf. It moves as a dreamy soundscape with delicate vocals and a subtle psychedelic undertone. Angelic colour comes through Lois’s layered harmonies in the backing track, while the lead carries driving, reflective and reassuring lyrics, like going back in time to give yourself advice. Plucked pizzicato strings, a dark aching synth, electric guitar plucks and a trip hop like beat shape the palette. As a Night Wolf hallmark in their work together, a harder beat arrives right at the end to bring a sense of panic and power to the close. The blend feels calm and dreamlike yet purposeful, a distinctive step for a well versed duo. text: "They say people can fall in love in just 4 minutes—if the connection is deep enough." This love fact inspired me to create... "They say people can fall in love in just 4 minutes—if the connection is deep enough." This love fact inspired me to create this song. With the sounds of 90s Synthwave, Retropop, and Citypop, it brought a touch of brightness back to the melancholic world of Before Morning Comes. After several months and many stories shared through my music, I felt a longing to revisit my own roots. That’s how this Extended Version was born. I’ve poured more detail into the production, refining every synth and beat while hoping to keep the soul of the original track alive. Please take a listen... If you still find yourself falling for this melody, I’d be so grateful if you could Like and Subscribe. I might not be able to post as often as I’d like because of my full-time job, but my love for making music never fades. Consider your support the fuel that keeps my creative fire burning. tags: album review|music|se so neon|indie music|alternative music
summary: SE SO NEON's : Scattered Every Now and Then
text: SE SO NEON's : Scattered Every Now and Then SE SO NEON's <NOW>: Scattered Every Now and Then Artwork from Apple Music // TL;DR: SE SO NEON's <NOW> delivers introspective, wonderfully-crafted pop and rock numbers that trip over themselves in the context of an album. There’s a fragmented sensation that pervades much of <NOW> , the first full-length project by then-band now-solo project SE SO NEON, helmed by Korean indie prodigy So!YoOn! [sic] Up until this point, she had been able to delineate the strong-willed and bombastic rock escapades of SSN from the more soulful and introspective indie-pop atmospheres of her solo work, and despite being down every other full-time member besides their figurehead, <NOW> still manages to maintain somewhat of an independent space for itself, though it shows a bit of a struggle in deciding how it wants to present SSN holistically, with two delineated moods that bring to mind distinct sides of SSN—all without the curated harmony of their previous EPs. The record’s opening three tracks—its main singles—stick the closest to the pop atmospheres of So!YoOn!’s solo work, yet they remain wistfully blissful and euphonic while keeping intact the atmospheres of SSN’s traditional purview of tranquility (i.e., Winter ). Moments that sometimes trail off into a void of auditory fog (e.g., Eden , p and q ) are violently juxtaposed by upbeat, head-turning rockers that, despite being drenched with more polish and softening than their previous EPs might have recommended. Tracks like Now , 3 Revolution , and the rerecording of Kidd still demand heavy attention brought to its pounding and urgent atmospheres, swirling from deceptively unassuming pickup points to wall-climbing crescendos. The struggle on <NOW> is that these two very delineated sides of SSN’s brand have been so firmly established with much gusto and zest that it makes for a rather convoluted listen, where these two halves are haphazardly wedded to form a listen that sometimes trips over itself when deciding how it wants listeners to feel. (Oddly enough, the tracks debuted 3-4 years ago mesh more nicely with their mood-brethren than the respective halves of the record.) While not much of a problem for the playlist-driven landscapes of today, So!YoOn!’s prior attention to detail would have done wonders to better curate the integration of SSN’s distinct halves, rather than leave <NOW> a high-quality but sometimes clumsy “choose your own adventure” game. RECOMMENDED TRACKS: Remember!, Now, Secret Police, Kidd, 3 Revolution 26/03/25 // SE SO NEON's <NOW>: Scattered Every Now and Then Artwork from Apple Music // TL;DR: SE SO NEON's <NOW> delivers introspective, wonderfully-crafted pop and rock numbers that trip over themselves in the context of an album. With increasing demand for flexible, skill-based learning, students across age groups are actively searching for qualified teachers — especially for options like an online Canvas Painting Course Pune or even oil painting classes in Pune delivered virtually. For tutors, this shift opens doors to global reach, scalable income, and long-term career stability. What Are Online Painting Classes? Online painting classes are live or structured virtual sessions where art tutors teach painting techniques using video platforms, digital tools, and guided practice. Whether it’s a beginner enrolling in an online Canvas Painting Course Pune or someone exploring oil painting classes in Pune , students can now learn from anywhere without physical studio limitations. Why Online Painting Classes Are in High Demand Most blogs focus on learning painting online, but tutors are actively searching for income opportunities. Demand is rising because: Students prefer learning from home Parents look for safe, flexible classes for children Adults want creative hobbies alongside work Access to quality oil painting classes in Pune is still limited locally Online painting classes solve all these problems at once by offering accessibility and flexibility. How Online Painting Classes Create New Income Streams for Art Tutors 1. Lower Costs, Higher Profit Margins Traditional painting classes require: Studio rent Art material setup Travel and maintenance expenses By shifting to online painting classes , tutors can: Teach from home Run multiple batches Increase net income Lower expenses automatically lead to higher profitability per student. 3. Yes, painting can be taught effectively online when classes are: Live and interactive Structured by skill level Supported with clear visual demonstrations Recorded for revision Many students enrolled in online painting classes or even virtual oil painting classes in Pune perform better because they can learn at their own pace. What Types of Painting Classes Work Best Online? Wider Student Audience for Online Painting Tutors Online painting classes attract: Children learning art fundamentals Teenagers building portfolios Adults exploring creative hobbies Working professionals seeking stress relief Homemakers restarting creative careers This wide audience ensures steady demand for online painting classes throughout the year. How many students per batch? Ideally 6–10 students for better attention. summary: the spotify algorithm doesn't care about your talent. it cares about this.
text: the spotify algorithm doesn't care about your talent. it cares about this. the spotify algorithm doesn't care about your talent. it cares about this. you could write the best song released this year and spotify won't push it to a single new listener if the numbers behind it don't add up. that's not an exaggeration. that's how the system is built. the algorithm doesn't evaluate music quality. it doesn't listen to your track and think "wow, that bridge is incredible." what it does is measure behavior. saves. completion rates. how many followers actually stream your release in the first 72 hours. whether new listeners come back or bounce after 15 seconds. here's the part that broke my brain when i finally understood it: an artist with 800 engaged followers will get more algorithmic push than someone with 20,000 dead ones. because spotify calculates ratios, not totals. if 80% of your follower base streams your new drop within a week, the system reads that as "this artist's audience is locked in" and expands your reach. if 3% of your followers bother to press play, it reads that as "nobody cares" and moves on. this is why buying bot followers is career suicide. you're not just wasting money. you're actively destroying your engagement ratio, which is the one metric that determines whether the algorithm works for you or against you. and this is also why building a real follower base matters more than almost anything else you can do as an independent artist. not followers as a vanity number. followers as a distribution network. every real follower with an active spotify account is a guaranteed impression on release radar every time you drop something new. that's not marketing fluff. that's literally how the infrastructure works. platforms like ss market exist specifically for this. real spotify followers from active accounts who actually use the platform. not bots. not empty profiles. people who listen. → ss market — spotify followers the game isn't "make better music and hope someone notices." the game is "make great music AND build the engagement infrastructure that forces the algorithm to notice." one without the other gets you nowhere. the spotify summary: Hoping to Find
text: Hoping to Find Hoping to Find Find A Song that is like the space between being awake and being asleep Empty Melon - Hoping to Find Born in Montreal’s DIY scene, Empty Melon (the solo project of Ada Lea bassist/producer Summer Kodama) introduces her debut single “Hoping to Find,” a cinematic, mellow, and emotionally introspective track that drifts between consciousness and dream. Rooted in ambient folk textures and experimental sensibility, the song captures a moment of creative reclamation and quiet self-renewal. What sets “Hoping to Find” apart is its atmosphere. “The song is sort of like the space between being awake and being asleep,” she says. “It sits in a corner of my brain that allowed me to do whatever I wanted, in accordance with the feelings I held without judgement or parameters.” That in-between quality defines the track’s tone: cinematic yet intimate, ambient yet grounded in organic instrumentation. Added to FAS Spotify playlist indie folk/country/americana . When you finally accept it's over with the love of your life, it's time to play "Mariposa". A song about flying to your dream. Flying to a better future. Flying to find happiness. And it's one of @cadencewishes all-time best vocal performances and post-productions (the way she echos her voice in the opposite channel for emotional effect is brillaint). That's why I love her music. Sometimes it feels like she's singing for me. It can feel like she's singing for you too. summary: Influence Without Credit? Queens of the Stone Age and the Shape of Modern Rock
text: Influence Without Credit? Queens of the Stone Age and the Shape of Modern Rock Influence Without Credit? Queens of the Stone Age and the Shape of Modern Rock Queens of the Stone Age occupy a peculiar cultural blind spot. They headline festivals, their albums are permanently canonised and their influence is broadly acknowledged yet they’re rarely framed as the band that shaped how modern rock looks, moves and behaves. Instead, they sit just outside the ‘greats’, their impact scattered across scenes absorbed so deeply into them it’s almost invisible. That invisibility is precisely why I started seeing them everywhere. I felt it most recently while watching a performance by Wolf Alice ahead of their just-announced Finsbury Park ‘Homecoming’ show. Beyond the obvious music lineage, there was a stark physical resemblance between Theo Ellis and Michael Shuman. Not just in look (and the pitchfork tattoo plastered onto Ellis’ arm), but also in stance. The grounded posture, the economy of movement and the sense that the bassline was carrying the song’s emotional weight rather than embellishing it. It wasn’t imitation, it was inheritance shown as a form of admiration and respect that the band displayed frequently, even supporting them on their 2018 tour. Once you notice it, it becomes hard to ignore. The Queen's influence doesn’t always announce itself through clear distortion or tempo; it rather reveals itself through discipline and restraint. And increasingly, that influence has trickled into youth culture without ever being detected as their own. Teenagers in the 2020s who are picking up guitars and building bands are absorbing QOTSA’s approach through Wolf Alice, Inhaler, Arctic Monkeys and Biffy Clyro without knowing the blueprint was laid by Homme and his bandmates - its influence without credit is cultural osmosis at its finest. Queens of the Stone Age emerged from a lineage far removed from the indie scenes they would later infiltrate. Rooted in Josh Homme’s desert rock origins with his group Kyuss, the band fused proto-punk nihilism with heavy metal minimalism and integrated blues repetition into something sleek and controlled. The Stooges, Black Sabbath, Black Flag and ZZ Top are all present in their music, yet muted enough for Homme to replace chaos with precision, creating music that felt industrial without being cold. The band creates something heavy without it being bloated, this approach making QOTSA endlessly adaptable, with Songs for the Deaf not only defining a sound but establishing a fundamental framework where vocals float above the machinery rather than leading it. That framework quietly seeped into later Foo Fighters projects with Dave Grohl stating on the Bill Simmons show that queens are “the best rock band in the world. Like nobody even gets close” and the wider mainstreaming of muscular, rhythm-led alternative rock. But the more interesting legacy is how that framework shows up in bands that don’t sound like Queens at all. Take Inhaler. Watching bassist Robert Keating, there’s a familiar Queens physicality at play with minimal movement, locked-in repetition, even bleached blonde hair and stylistic choices. Ellis (of Wolf Alice) and Keating share an understanding that tension is built by staying in place rather than constantly escalating like Shuman preached. It’s the same visual language philosophy that prioritises control over release. A behavioural borrowing and how they didn’t just influence records, they additionally influenced how bands occupy space, how they stand on stage, how they allow rhythm sections to lead without spectacle rather than be pushed toward a climax now internalized for a younger generation of fans, often teenagers hearing it as natural rather than borrowed, never realising the origin point. Despite all this, QOTSA are rarely positioned as ‘greats’ of rock. Part of that is due to timing. They're seen as too heavy for early indie romanticism yet too restrained for metal maximalism. While The Strokes recentered indie around New York cool and Arctic Monkeys became the representative of British youth, Queens remained geographically and culturally unmoored and impossible to pinpoint. Influential but uninterested in ownership. Seeing them live and meeting them at Way Out West in Gothenburg only reinforced that feeling. Even then, as a kid, it was clear they weren’t chasing mythology. They felt present, human, oddly understated and unbothered for a band whose fingerprints were already everywhere. So are Queens of the Stone Age underrated? Yes, not in terms of acclaim but culturally, their influence is diffused instead of declarative, shaping the next generation of bands and listeners, even if most don’t realise they’re walking in QOTSA’s shadow from passed down influence and fan bases of these younger artists. They didn’t define a generation by standing at its centre, they defined it by quietly teaching everyone else. Rooted in Josh Homme’s desert rock origins with his group Kyuss, the band fused proto-punk nihilism with heavy metal minimalism and integrated blues repetition into something sleek and controlled. The Stooges, Black Sabbath, Black Flag and ZZ Top are all present in their music, yet muted enough for Homme to replace chaos with precision, creating music that felt industrial without being cold. The band creates something heavy without it being bloated, this approach making QOTSA endlessly adaptable, with Songs for the Deaf not only defining a sound but establishing a fundamental framework where vocals float above the machinery rather than leading it. That framework quietly seeped into later Foo Fighters projects with Dave Grohl stating on the Bill Simmons show that queens are “the best rock band in the world. Like nobody even gets close” and the wider mainstreaming of muscular, rhythm-led alternative rock. But the more interesting legacy is how that framework shows up in bands that don’t sound like Queens at all. Take Inhaler. Watching bassist Robert Keating, there’s a familiar Queens physicality at play with minimal movement, locked-in repetition, even bleached blonde hair and stylistic choices. Ellis (of Wolf Alice) and Keating share an understanding that tension is built by staying in place rather than constantly escalating like Shuman preached. It’s the same visual language philosophy that prioritises control over release. A behavioural borrowing and how they didn’t just influence records, they additionally influenced how bands occupy space, how they stand on stage, how they allow rhythm sections to lead without spectacle rather than be pushed toward a climax now internalized for a younger generation of fans, often teenagers hearing it as natural rather than borrowed, never realising the origin point. Despite all this, QOTSA are rarely positioned as ‘greats’ of rock. Part of that is due to timing. They're seen as too heavy for early indie romanticism yet too restrained for metal maximalism. While The Strokes recentered indie around New York cool and Arctic Monkeys became the representative of British youth, Queens remained geographically and culturally unmoored and impossible to pinpoint. Influential but uninterested in ownership. Seeing them live and meeting them at Way Out West in Gothenburg only reinforced that feeling. Even then, as a kid, it was clear they weren’t chasing mythology. They felt present, human, oddly understated and unbothered for a band whose fingerprints were already everywhere. So are Queens of the Stone Age underrated? Yes, not in terms of acclaim but culturally, their influence is diffused instead of declarative, shaping the next generation of bands and listeners, even if most don’t realise they’re walking in QOTSA’s shadow from passed down influence and fan bases of these younger artists. They didn’t define a generation by standing at its centre, they defined it by quietly teaching everyone else. summary: The Scrapped Swans Fury Album(s)
text: The Scrapped Swans Fury Album(s) The Scrapped Swans Fury Album(s) A working album cover for the now scrapped "Lost To Not Be Found" EP. Swans Fury originally started as nothing more than a recording project for me to start building a sort of music portfolio, where I could post any original song concepts as I learned a DAW. I needed an artist name, and where "Swans Fury" came from is a genuine mystery to me. I think it might be a play on "Lambs Anger" by Mr. Oizo, and the concept of a nature theme, particularly related to nature's more than appropriate disdain towards humanity, made sense to the first things I wanted to write about. Those themes of nature would most certainly have been inspired by The Microphones, particularly an album called "The Glow Pt. 2" The first album I thought about would've been similar to the lengthy titles that Teen Suicide likes to use, in the vein of We found two dead swans and filled their bodies with flowers and I Will Be My Own Hell Because There Is a Devil Inside My Body , following the aformentioned nature theme. One day, we saw a cat that had passed away beyond the sidewalk. Not quite in our front yard, not in the neighbors', but an awkward spot in between both of our homes. She looked quite sick before she passed, so both my family and the neighbors unwillingly strayed away from burying her, choosing to call Animal Control to do so properly. They never came, and over the course of a couple of days to a few weeks, I watched as her corpse slowly wittled away, leaving a cat-shaped shadow where the grass didn't grow for a while. It reminded me of the shadows left behind after the nuclear detonations over Hiroshima and Nagasaki, while also making me think about the cycle of life, and how I eased my guilt by thinking about how her body served as food for the soil, continuing the natural circle of life on an Earth otherwise paved over by humanity. My notes app tells me I was going to call the album: Here lies the mother of three kittens, she died in the blistering heat over 115 degrees Fahrenheit, animal control was called five times but never showed up, and now her body feeds the soil , but not before she left a shadow on her final place of rest. Her Body Feeds The Soil would be the shortened title, with the full title being written out on the cover, composited over a picture of that patch of grass. some of the song names I came up with were: the perverse law of nature we are animals her body feeds the soil the swans lament (might be the source of swans fury) tears in rain i have a recurring dream where I watch the bombs fall over Los Angeles such small people on the great stage of the world and PREY ANIMALS When I eventually moved on from that first concept, I narrowed in on an angsty punk record about nature's retaliation against humanity. That idea was all I got. No songs came of this tiny era, but a few lyrics about as explicitly satirical as 'neet freak' were in my notes. Stuff about "i drive a big truck and mother nature is my bitch". Like. I was not subtle about it. W e'll Be Data One Day was an idea I had, with themes that I still want to explore, so I won't dive too far into them. It ran through a couple of iterations, revolving around the narrative of two lovers who need to escape into the internet through digital recreations of themselves/uploaded consciousness. What would've been the final cover of Lost To Not Be Found Simply put, Lost To Not Be Found was about skipping town, leaving everything behind, but hating the city you found yourself in. The name and thematic nature is inspired by Car Seat Headrest's How To Leave Town , but i'll be very honest, I hadn't listened to that album at the time, so i LARP'd my way through inspiration just from the idea of it alone. It was sonically most inspired by Beach House's song Alien , and in retrospect, is basically "change the homework a little bit so it's not obvious." This album had two "complete" songs to it, which are now scrapped. I call them scrapped because this was the last "album" i worked on before I switched DAW's entirely, from Bandlab's Cakewalk to Reaper. All of the projects before this point are essentially gone as Cakewalk chose to lock itself behind a paywall, and the conversion process into the new Bandlab Sonar program is not an effort I'm willing to make and money i'm willing to pay to save a few scrapped songs in a program I've outgrown. You're Closer Than You Think I never took this concept seriously, just got the idea for a cool album cover. I even posted this cover on reddit, so it's up for grabs. Shoot a cred to the ol Swans Fury if you do, tho. What followed was 'neet freak' But I'd like to save that development process for it's own thread. summary: music update 3/18/26
text: music update 3/18/26 music update 3/18/26 as of yesterday I believe I have officially finished the recording of the second album "SINK INTO YOURSELF" which acts as a kind of sequel to the 'neet freak' EP. the tagline of this record is: THE PREGAME, THE PARTY, AND THE PARANOIA where we're at there's a couple of vocal takes and instrument tracks that I wanna redo, but I may be too lazy and not have the chance to do them,, especially vocals. i still live with my folks. im quite shy. overall, the album is about 90% done. I've never been inclined to make an album sound cohesive, so to speak. 'neet freak' jumps from a pretty bouncy lofi drum rock track, over to straight messy punk rock, then into some math rock influence, before going acoustic and then finishing with a big, angsty, noise finale. that is to say i never shyed away from keeping to one solid sound, especially when narratively it works to better represent the fluctuations of emotion that come from an unwell teenager. That being said, I feel like some of the tracks on Sink Into Yourself feel a little out there, and not in a way that particularly blends in well the way I believe most tracks do on neet freak. even though neet freak jumps around, it still cohesive in a way, being MAINLY high energy, with a couple of dips. Sink Into Yourself does jump around a bit, in subject matter, in energy, and in overall tonality. The first track i wrote and recorded is "The Night Is Young," a track that was going to be the title of the record before I wrote Sink Into Yourself and felt that better embodied the narrative I wanted to tell. Because I was jumping straight out of 'neet freak' when I wrote that song, it feels the most in line with what most of you are familiar with out of me. It is also recorded in a way that has made it quite difficult to mix and master in a way that makes it sound like its part of the same album as the other tracks. Writing and recording the rest of the album gave me a lot of practice and let me build a few different presets for instruments, and thus the rest of the tracks are pretty consistent, while this one sticks out like a sore thumb. If worst comes to absolute worst, I will re-record that track, but I think and hope I can make it work. My biggest fear which has started to become a reality is that I will listen to these tracks and start to get sick of them, thinking I can put out better. If i let that happen, this album wont come out lol. 'neet freak' was not what I first had in mind for my debut album. I had another record in concept, but I'll talk about that in another post. The relevance is that I let myself sit on those songs too long, hoping to come up with more tracks to fill out a setlist, and then let those songs become outdated in my head, and ended up scrapped. It happened a little bit with 'neet freak' but I let myself publish that album before I started to hate it. I feel like my writing and style changed over the process of writing Sink Into Yourself, so the first songs I wrote feel outdated to me. That won't be true of anyone listening, so I'll try and not let that get in the way. I hope to finish mixing soon in a way that will make me love these songs again, before I start thinking I should scrap them. so long story short Sink Into Yourself is on the way! I am quite close to the completion of this next album, which comes out to 6 new original tracks. For all except a couple of instruments and vocals, we have officially entered the mixing and mastering stages. It won't be long after that we get a release date. summary: Supporting Role???
text: Supporting Role??? Supporting Role??? Find A Song about being someone's option instead of their priority Arlesque - Supporting Role??? "Supporting Role" is the kind of song that hits you right in the chest when you've ever loved someone who only let you stand in the wings. It's raw, shimmering heartbreak-pop that turns the pain of being someone's option—not their priority—into something strangely beautiful and empowering. With aching melodies, intimate vocals that feel whispered straight to the listener, and production that builds from quiet vulnerability to cathartic release, the track captures that devastating moment when you realize you're writing yourself out of your own story just to fit into theirs. This isn't just a breakup song; it's a reclamation. "Supporting Role" is a reminder that the spotlight was always meant for the person brave enough to step out of the shadows—even if it means walking away. summary: 14/03/26 gig report
text: 14/03/26 gig report 14/03/26 gig report I played my 3rd professional show today and as usual I went into it hoping that this one would be some kind of breakthrough moment where some missing piece would finally lock into place and we'd play amazing. Everyone told me we sounded great, but looking at the few videos I've seen so far, it was ok, maybe even a bit shit to be honest. It's hard to say with my music because I think a lot of the 'wow' factor just comes from the songs being unusual and engaging so the performance mindset is kinda "hold on for dear life and hope it sounds good enough to allow the songs to shine". This gig was a bit different in that we played with two electric guitars, standing up. As opposed to our usual setup: me on acoustic, my bandmate playing a tambourine, melodica and about 2 other instruments, sitting down, plus vocals. So we were a lot more exposed. However even though this wasn't our swan song, I think it was probably the best we've played. Numerous friends came up to me and looked me dead in the eye and said things like "dude, I know I said your last gig was good but that was just *mwah*" Which i take as outside of the normal threshold of supporting your friends doing creative stuff. Also I have to mention that the BASSIST from FUCKING DC FONTAINES was there, and said something along the lines of "yeah that type of music is hard to make work live" but also that he thought we were the best act that night so idk. Idk what to think anymore, im drinking wine in my room trying to assess all this shit. I guess it was fine, I'll see you next time. 14/03/26 gig report I played my 3rd professional show today and as usual I went into it hoping that this one would be some kind of breakthrough moment where some missing piece would finally lock into place and we'd play amazing. This gig was a bit different in that we played with two electric guitars, standing up. As opposed to our usual setup: me on acoustic, my bandmate playing a tambourine, melodica and about 2 other instruments, sitting down, plus vocals. So we were a lot more exposed. However even though this wasn't our swan song, I think it was probably the best we've played. Numerous friends came up to me and looked me dead in the eye and said things like "dude, I know I said your last gig was good but that was just *mwah*" Which i take as outside of the normal threshold of supporting your friends doing creative stuff. Also I have to mention that the BASSIST from FUCKING DC FONTAINES was there, and said something along the lines of "yeah that type of music is hard to make work live" but also that he thought we were the best act that night so idk. Idk what to think anymore, im drinking wine in my room trying to assess all this shit. I guess it was fine, I'll see you next time.

summary: Learning Japanese Day 1..
text: Learning Japanese Day 1.. Learning Japanese Day 1.. Hello people that probably don't follow me, Today I started learning the amazing and totally not hard language; Japanese, there are many reasons why I want to learn Japanese, not including the over-used "OmG GUYS!! i LoVe aNiMe I WaNnA LeaRm JAPan" that many people like to use as a valid reason, though I suppose my reasons are quite similar, aside from the fact I don't even like Anime very much 1. I've always been very fascinated by Japanese culture, something about it really appeals to me compared to what I'm used to in western culture. 2. Almost everything I've ever liked has come from Japan, from JRPG's like the Mother series and Chrono Trigger, to most music I've loved originating from there, I've always fallen in love with Japanese media almost instantly. 3. I'd like to sort of "pay respect"?? in a way to all the games I've loved that come from there, as most of you may know I'm a very passionate musician and programmer, most of my work has always been inspired by either some piece of Japanese media or Toby Fox, I'd like to make my game available to that sort of "audience" in a way, and I'd also like to make my game a JRPG, however I won't expand on this project right now, I can't make any promises for when you will see it... perhaps next month or next year... who knows. ANYWAYS I started memorizing Hiragana, currently the 5 basic characters that I'm pretty sure everyone is taught at first, あ, い, う, え and お I also started putting these characters together to form some very simple words, あい (love), うえ (up/above) and いいえ (no). I know it's not much but I'm very proud of what I've leaned so far, I might not update every single day but perhaps every week or so when I've learned something really major or something idk. Anyways that's all I have uhhhhhhhhhhh Enjoy this cool song I made inspired by Crumbling Tower from Deltarune Chapter 4. Approaching Doom... Learning Japanese Day 1.. summary: Song of the Day
text: Song of the Day Song of the Day Song: Maybe I'm the Reason Artist: Dev Lemons EP: Maybe I'm the Reason / Be Cool Genre: hyperpop/bedroom pop I came across this song on accident and boy oh boy am I thankful it found me! This song is so irresistibly catchy that I can't stop listening to it. Even though though the song is melancholic, I can't help wanting to dance to the beat. The mellowness eventually leads up to a rocking finish. I like the way the lyrics are sung like the singer is talking to herself. It makes what she's saying feel more relatable. Dev Lemons is a fairly small artist with about 160k listeners on Spotify. I'm gonna be checking out her other work in hopes she has more bangers like this! She also has a music video to this song on YouTube.
