summary: 9. choke enough - Oklou
text: 9. choke enough - Oklou 9. choke enough - Oklou I was lucky enough to catch Oklou’s set at Melbourne Laneway Festival a few weeks ago. I had been hearing a lot of praise for her music recently, and was told by friends that I should definitely take the chance to see her, so I knew not to miss her performance. I tried to prepare a little by listening to a few of Oklou’s songs prior to the concert, but I was mostly unfamiliar with her music. I enjoyed the energy of the concert a lot, so now felt like the perfect time to properly listen to her recent album choke enough. I was struggling to decide on which new album to listen to this week, but the moment I put this album on , I knew it was exactly what I needed right now. With choke enough, Oklou handles pop and electronic music in a way that is experimental, ethereal and subtle. The album feels futuristic, but also strangely ancient at the same time – it almost feels like playing a medieval video game with the sound journey it takes you on. The whimsical melodies in songs such as ‘thank you for recording’ sound just as folky as they sound alien. I loved the meeting of these influences and the way they created such a distinctive sound in this album. One of my favourite aspects of choke enough is how calming I found it to listen to. Even the more dancey songs such as ‘harvest sky’ feel gentle whilst still being upbeat, and I think this intriguing duality is an impressive feat to achieve. At first listen, this album felt almost too quiet and understated, but the more I listened the more I loved the space that Oklou creates in her songs. The minimalistic sound of choke enough somehow allows the detail of each song to shine even more – there is plenty of room to appreciate the neat, magical melodies, otherworldly synths, and thrumming, heartbeat-like bass and drums. In summary, choke enough is a beautiful, impressionistic, indie pop album that I will definitely be returning to. I’m really glad I got the opportunity to witness her live show, as I feel like this enhanced my experience listening to the album even more. Favourite songs: blade bird, harvest sky, endless, family and friends, obvious Favourite moments: The chorus of ‘family and friends’ – this song has such a beautiful sense of buildup, urgency and emotion in the lyric delivery and layering of sounds. The deep, resonant drones and bass of ‘endless’ – such an atmospheric way to start the album, this track really draws you in. The title ‘endless’ in combination with these sounds makes me think of floating in space. The increase in energy when the album reaches ‘harvest sky’ – the more clubby vibe of this song is so catchy and makes for a great contrast with the rest of the album, while still feeling consistent. The acoustic guitar elements of ‘blade bird’ – they work so well combined with the more electronic beats, and I think this song is such a perfect, peaceful way for the album to end. 9. choke enough - Oklou I was lucky enough to catch Oklou’s set at Melbourne Laneway Festival a few weeks ago. summary: Ecos del Presente: cuatro viajes sonoros entre introspección, ruptura y energía urbana
text: Ecos del Presente: cuatro viajes sonoros entre introspección, ruptura y energía urbana Ecos del Presente: cuatro viajes sonoros entre introspección, ruptura y energía urbana Oski XD Doski – "Bonny Wraith" With "Bonny Wraith," Oski XD Doski kicks off their album Latency Issues with an energy that sits right in that sweet spot between post-punk nostalgia and the laid-back vibe of surf rock. The track starts off with these jittery, angular guitar riffs that quickly open up into a driving, vibrant beat—giving the band’s alternative spirit plenty of room to play around with different textures and moods. The song feels a bit like a coastal road trip under a gray sky: it’s got that post-punk melancholy in the melodies, but there’s a bright, steady pulse pushing it forward. This mix creates a dynamic sound that isn't afraid to jump between styles while still keeping its own unique identity. As the lead single for Latency Issues , "Bonny Wraith" perfectly captures the heart of the project: music born out of friendship, shaped by uncertain times, and built to be an emotional escape. The result is a fresh, slightly quirky track that proves alt-rock can still reinvent itself without losing its soul. summary: I'm currently making my first official break-up album
text: I'm currently making my first official break-up album I'm currently making my first official break-up album Last month, I started making nine instrumental tracks for my new record and this upcoming Thursday I'm going to start recording vocals!!!!!! Since after I broke up with someone last December, I've been writing new songs. While I've been coping with the break-up, I've been listening to a lot of Lorde, especially her debut album, Pure Heroine, which made me want to create an "adult Pure Heroine". I also took inspiration from my own earlier work, including my 2020 debut album, Melrose. I'm so excited to share new music with all after I'm finished with it... and it might be done around July or September. summary: A-T-6 012 Throwing Muses
text: A-T-6 012 Throwing Muses A-T-6 012 Throwing Muses It's March and I don't think I've posted any indie music from 1986 yet. That not technically correct. Almost all the music I've shared this year was originally put out by an independent record label, it's just not considered as having that indie sound By 1986 The Smiths were the face of independent music in the UK, and they are the initial reason the indie scene would become synonymous with jangle pop. The seminal band had been achieving mainstream success since 1983, and they stood out against the other artists in the pages of Smash Hits and Number One . Morrissey was indie's first icon. British youth culture of the mid-eighties saw fewer people styling themselves on Robert Smith and more quiffs, cardigans, NHS glasses, and rolled up jeans. The Smiths were to define the look and the sound of indie. This is why in the public's imagination someone like Kylie was never seen as an indie artist despite being signed to independent record labels for the first decade of her career After the arrival of the landmark C86 compilation in 1986 the indie genre is solidified as predominantly guitar music of the jangly variety. C86 was a cassette compilation first available by mail order from NME , then Rough Trade later released it as an LP. At the time there was a war going on in the offices of NME between journalists who were fans of hip hop and the established rock traditionalists. C86 was a campaign by the traditionalists to keep the publication as, first and foremost, a newspaper that reports on guitar based music. And this is what happened. The C86 comp has been massively overhyped over the years and doesn't need me to say anything else about it I don't know a lot about the US scene but college rock was in its heyday in the middle of the eighties. I don't think alternative rock was used as a term until later. Billboard's Alternative Airplay chart began in late 88 Throwing Muses were formed by step-sisters, Kristin Hersh and Tanya Donelly, in Newport, Rhode Island, USA. They move to Boston in 1985 where they recorded their debut album, it's little more than a demo, which came to be known as the “The Doghouse” tape. The "doghouse" refers to either the building the band are posing in front of in the photograph on the tape cover, or, the nickname Hersh gave the Providence apartment where she wrote much of the material. The album's producer and tireless champion of the Boston scene, Gary Smith, sends the cassette out far and wide. This is how it comes to the attention of Ivo Watts-Russell, co-founder of the 4AD, in London, England Throwing Muses are the first American act to sign with 4AD. Hersh recalls, "I signed with them [4AD] because Ivo was funny and goofy, and that was about it." Their signing breaks the ice for fellow Boston band The Pixies - who 40-years later have joined The Ramones and Joy Division as alternative acts marked as safe by mainstream retailers, broadcasters, journalists, and algorithms, pushing all other great music into the shadows. Focus on a a handful of artists maximises profits. Unlike The Pixies, Throwing Muses aren't going to sell many t-shirts in supermarkets or fast fashion outlets. Parents and the children they bank role through life can agree The Pixies were a great band, like Fleetwood Mac 4AD is a spin-off of one the big British independent record labels founded in the 1970s, Beggars Banquet. One of three sub-labels Beggars Banquet put up cash for and distributed at the beginning of the eighties Some of the other big Indies from punk/post-punk era were Rough Trade, Cherry Red, Factory, Stiff, Fiction, and Mute. By the eighties these labels had become institutions but retained some of their DIY charm, this is in contrast with 1960s indies such as Chrysalis (upto 1985) and Island Records. 4AD began big. After releasing their debut single "Bela Lugosi's Dead" they signed goth pioneers Bauhaus and brought The Birthday Party to audiences outside of Australia. The 4AD versions were produced by Gil Norton. Up to this point Norton had mainly worked with acts from his hometown of Liverpool - where I live - China Crisis, The Pale Fountains, Echo & The Bunnymen. That not technically correct. Almost all the music I've shared this year was originally put out by an independent record label, it's just not considered as having that indie sound By 1986 The Smiths were the face of independent music in the UK, and they are the initial reason the indie scene would become synonymous with jangle pop. The seminal band had been achieving mainstream success since 1983, and they stood out against the other artists in the pages of Smash Hits and Number One . Morrissey was indie's first icon. British youth culture of the mid-eighties saw fewer people styling themselves on Robert Smith and more quiffs, cardigans, NHS glasses, and rolled up jeans. The Smiths were to define the look and the sound of indie. This is why in the public's imagination someone like Kylie was never seen as an indie artist despite being signed to independent record labels for the first decade of her career After the arrival of the landmark C86 compilation in 1986 the indie genre is solidified as predominantly guitar music of the jangly variety. C86 was a cassette compilation first available by mail order from NME , then Rough Trade later released it as an LP. At the time there was a war going on in the offices of NME between journalists who were fans of hip hop and the established rock traditionalists. C86 was a campaign by the traditionalists to keep the publication as, first and foremost, a newspaper that reports on guitar based music. And this is what happened. The C86 comp has been massively overhyped over the years and doesn't need me to say anything else about it I don't know a lot about the US scene but college rock was in its heyday in the middle of the eighties. text: Ascentra - Ascentra - New York To Singapore This track is a bit of an experiment for me. I usually stay inside my usual genre... This track is a bit of an experiment for me. I usually stay inside my usual genre lane, but with New York to Singapore I wanted to step outside of that and try something different. It leans into an experimental mix of chillstep and dubstep textures, kind of like a late-night journey between two cities on opposite sides of the world. For this one I relied heavily on samples—chopping, layering, and reshaping them to build the atmosphere and movement of the track. It was a different workflow for me, but honestly a really fun one. Think of it like traveling from the neon streets of New York to the humid night skyline of Singapore… same trip, different moods along the way. This is my first attempt at pushing beyond my usual style, so let me know what you think. 🎧 Headphones recommended. This track is a bit of an experiment for me. summary: Women in Rock – Monthly Highlight: Ellie Rowsell
text: Women in Rock – Monthly Highlight: Ellie Rowsell Women in Rock – Monthly Highlight: Ellie Rowsell If you've tuned into British indie or alternative rock over the last decade, Ellie Rowsell's raw melodic voice has likely pierced through. As Wolf Alice's frontwoman, lead guitarist and songwriter, she's not just participating in rock's revival but steering it into an era where women don't orbit the stage but own it. Wolf Alice kicked off humbly in 2010 in North London, Rowsell and guitarist Joff Oddie linking via MySpace for acoustic open mic gigs, originating as a folk duo that transformed into a full band. Wolf Alice named themselves after a Tori Amos story, signalling literary depth amid the noise that was deemed ‘trendy’ in the era of sleaze. Their sound a unique combination of grunge's snarling distortion that crashes into dream pops ethereal haze flipping from whisper quiet introspection such as “Last Man on the Earth” to arena roars with “Yuk Foo” throughout a single album. My Love Is Cool was the start of the four-piece success. Singles like "Moaning Lisa Smile" and "Bros" buzzed online while the full LP detonated with critics raving over its genre-morphing composition. Quickly, fans packed sweaty venues, and suddenly Wolf Alice were touring relentlessly and headlining festivals. The coronation arrived with the band's sophomore album Visions of a Life in 2018, where the 12-track album clinched the Mercury Prize, beating Arctic Monkeys, Florence + the Machine, and Lily Allen. "You have to overcome hurdles being a woman in a band," Rowsell reflected post-win, nodding to the scepticism she'd faced as a female guitarist fronting the rock fray. It wasn't luck, but rather validation of her command, penning lyrics that dissect love's mess, while playing riffs that bite. Blue Weekend, released in 2012 inhanced the band's intimate grandeur with Rowsell later confessing, "I was afraid of showing people something that was worse than what came before.” However, despite her doubt, it went on to earn Grammy nods and cemented their global success amid pandemic lockdowns and by 2025's The Clearing, they'd honed a leaner edge in their most assured statement, drawing from their folk roots. They rewarded their innovative sound at the 2026 BRIT Awards, winning Group of the Year, where Rowsell's acceptance speech evangelised grassroots venues, stating them as "the lifeblood for bands like us starting out", emphasising their importance over corporate gloss. Rowsell's quiet radicalism shines in her confidence and honesty on womanhood in rock. "Seeing a woman centre stage with a guitar can be powerful," she's said, urging girls to grab instruments unapologetically challenging the genre's male gatekept lore where leads strum solos and steer ships. Women rocked edges in the past with Siouxsie Sioux's punk poetry and Courtney Love's riot grrrl rage; however, Rowsell mainstreams the full package through her alchemy of crafting timeless bangers while overcoming barriers that were previously unseen. Younger players now eye Wolf Alice not as an exception, but as a template; firm proof women blueprint rock's next wave. Quickly, fans packed sweaty venues, and suddenly Wolf Alice were touring relentlessly and headlining festivals. The coronation arrived with the band's sophomore album Visions of a Life in 2018, where the 12-track album clinched the Mercury Prize, beating Arctic Monkeys, Florence + the Machine, and Lily Allen. "You have to overcome hurdles being a woman in a band," Rowsell reflected post-win, nodding to the scepticism she'd faced as a female guitarist fronting the rock fray. It wasn't luck, but rather validation of her command, penning lyrics that dissect love's mess, while playing riffs that bite. Blue Weekend, released in 2012 inhanced the band's intimate grandeur with Rowsell later confessing, "I was afraid of showing people something that was worse than what came before.” However, despite her doubt, it went on to earn Grammy nods and cemented their global success amid pandemic lockdowns and by 2025's The Clearing, they'd honed a leaner edge in their most assured statement, drawing from their folk roots. They rewarded their innovative sound at the 2026 BRIT Awards, winning Group of the Year, where Rowsell's acceptance speech evangelised grassroots venues, stating them as "the lifeblood for bands like us starting out", emphasising their importance over corporate gloss. Rowsell's quiet radicalism shines in her confidence and honesty on womanhood in rock. "Seeing a woman centre stage with a guitar can be powerful," she's said, urging girls to grab instruments unapologetically challenging the genre's male gatekept lore where leads strum solos and steer ships. Women rocked edges in the past with Siouxsie Sioux's punk poetry and Courtney Love's riot grrrl rage; however, Rowsell mainstreams the full package through her alchemy of crafting timeless bangers while overcoming barriers that were previously unseen. Younger players now eye Wolf Alice not as an exception, but as a template; firm proof women blueprint rock's next wave. Written by Gia M-H Photographs by Harriet Bols, Nick Sayers and Andy Deluca

summary: BANDCAMP FRIDAY, SLUTS
text: BANDCAMP FRIDAY, SLUTS It’s motherfuckin BANDCAMP FRIDAY, SLUTS Chances are you already know what that means, but in case you don’t: Bandcamp is a platform where indie musicians like me put our music up for sale and music lovers like you can go buy it! And that’s *buy* not just add it to a playlist. Not rent it til the tech oligarchs decided to take it back. Fully BUY it to OWN. And the first Friday of certain months, Bandcamp waives their share of the revenue to let musicians make 100% of the money you spend! So hey, I’ve got like soooooooo much music in so many genres available to purchase. Most of it is Pay-what-you-can, meaning YOU decide the price that you can afford. And if you can’t afford anything, I get it. Me neither. You can set it to 0$. But if you can’t afford a few bucks, it makes the difference between my power bill getting paid or electricity being shut off. It makes the difference between me being able to keep making music or having to focus on other things. Truly, every little bit is appreciated. If you’re new to my music, I recommend checking out one of the following albums: Ganymede Gives Up The Ghost - a concept album about growing up. About queerness and disability. And about a trans superhero named Nuestra who fights to defend a city who rejects her. In The Retention Pond - My very first EP from 10 years ago! Songs about my home state of Florida, love in its many forms, and sacred rebellion. Beat The Virus 6: Curse of the Antivaxxers - I did a whole series of BTV albums during the height of COVID, but this is the one I wrote right after my friend died and it really captures the grief and weirdness of that time. Resolution - An album with a song for each month of the year! Topics ranging from Goblin warfare to the impermanence of friendships to necromancy to abortion. text: I think the reason I like indie music so much is because it feels real. Taylor Swift, Olivia Rodrigo, Sabrina Carpenter, Zara... I think the reason I like indie music so much is because it feels real. Taylor Swift, Olivia Rodrigo, Sabrina Carpenter, Zara Larsson (btw, she's a fellow swedish girlie) and all those popular, trendy artists, sure, they've made some good music but it's all for a record label, a company. Independant artists make their music independantally which means they can write and do whatever the fuck they want and naturally, that feels more natural, more real. summary: Old Songs for Kids by Family Grave
text: Old Songs for Kids by Family Grave Old Songs for Kids by Family Grave Terrific review for The Family Grave album 'Old Songs for Kids' by Bob Levoy at Oasis Entertatinment Blog. " From the opening track, the band leans into a stripped-down, emotionally direct sound. Guitars carry a gritty warmth—never overly polished, never buried under studio gloss. The production favors authenticity over perfection, allowing rough edges to remain intact. That choice works in the album’s favor, giving the songs a lived-in quality that feels honest rather than performative. Lyrically, Old Songs for Kids reads like stories told from the other side of experience. Themes of family tension, lost innocence, and generational cycles run throughout the record. The writing is sharp without being self-indulgent; introspective but never melodramatic. There’s a sense that these songs weren’t written to impress—they were written because they needed to be. ... What makes Old Songs for Kids compelling is its refusal to romanticize the past. Instead of nostalgia, the album offers clarity. It suggests that growing up doesn’t mean forgetting—it means understanding. In a musical landscape often dominated by surface-level hooks and quick consumption, The Family Grave has crafted something more enduring: a record that invites repeated listens and rewards attention. Old Songs for Kids isn’t about youth—it’s about what survives aft er it. " * * * * * Available now from Bandcamp http s: //thefamilygrave.bandcamp.com/album/old-songs-for-kids and on vinyl or CD via ElasticStage https ://elasticstage.com/thefamilygrave/releases/old-songs-for-kids-album Out on digital/streaming places this Friday, March 6th 2026. summary: Album Of The Week💿
text: Album Of The Week💿 Album Of The Week💿 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ One Album per Week for a whole year ―୨୧⋆ ˚ 8/52 ―୨୧⋆ ˚ rating: 5.5/10 -> Noise Between the Shades by Melting Palms Growing up in Germany was filled with multiple genres of music being pushed through being played on the radio or on niche events for certain folk directions. There was something for everyone, no matter how stark trends might shift throughout the years. However, the easier it got for people to make music, the more those 'easy listens' were pushed by radio stations. Being someone with a clear preference for post-punk and alternative rock music—besides the same three melancholy indie poster boys—there was never a clear landscape of artists to chose from. That will now change. Melting Palms is a psychedelic, post-punk, shoegaze, dreampop band from Hamburg, a city up the north. In their sophomore album, 'Noise Between the Shades', the collective perfectly capture the dreary and aery feeling of the seaport through 12 tracks spun over 54 minutes of an extravagant listening experience. As though on first listen, quite a few songs get swallowed by the length of the whole project and some parts seem repetitive towards the end, the record still holds up to being one of the top need to listen to albums in the alternative music scene. summary: What do you think about: Man Has Made Himself by Arthur Shea
text: What do you think about: Man Has Made Himself by Arthur Shea What do you think about: Man Has Made Himself by Arthur Shea Lyrics here. I've been listening to this song for a long ass time (mostly because I keep imagining an animation whenever I listen to it). This song feels like a dream like vision. I don't know whether to be scared and intimidated. It's maybe religious? The mentioning of sins ("beneath the sun until you rinse off the sins that follow you") points in that direction. The final line, "Something tells me god is gone, and man has made himself alone" supports this. For the most part, to me, it feels hopeless. A genuine "god is dead" moment from the singer; no rage, only a kind of empty hopelessness that I hope to never experience. The final line is just a statement of fact in the eyes of the singer, god is gone and man has built himself. (On second thought, "God is gone" could literally just mean that he left , that he abandoned humanity. Which is not even rage inducing, that's despair inducing.) As with all songs I include in my posts, the only way you're going to understand what I mean is by listening to the song yourself. It's only a minute and a half long. It's super short. summary: Mega post incoming! I have fallen into a bunch of new hyper focuses recently. I realise this has kind of happened as a bit of a...
text: Mega post incoming! I have fallen into a bunch of new hyper focuses recently. I realise this has kind of happened as a bit of a... Mega post incoming! I have fallen into a bunch of new hyper focuses recently. I realise this has kind of happened as a bit of a reaction to the times we are living in right now and to how I am living my own life too. Some months ago I began boycotting Spotify due to their now ex ceo investing heavily in AI weaponry. The platform itself also seems to be flooded with AI music and in doing that is further undercutting real human artists. As a result of this, I had been investing more in small musicians on Bandcamp. I also have started to collect physical format music again. I didn’t want to start doing CD’s again. Some years ago i had to part with a collection of 4000 which I had been collecting since I was a teenager due to space constraints. Same with Vinyl to a lesser extent. I have been big into Lofi, Synthwave and Vaporwave music for some time, which has a very nostalgic aesthetic. Those scenes seem to fetishise cassettes. So I decided to start collecting cassettes. However, I have a specific vision in mind with my cassette collection. I have been collecting 80’s electropop and also weird underground modern music. As a musician myself it has also given me ideas about how to combat both streaming and AI undercutting my musics worth. I am aiming to eventually put out some music on cassettes also. Another way AI has influenced me is to drag me away from making DAW based music. It can instantly generate almost anything i can make with softsynths and midi. While that isn’t going to prevent me from making music in my DAW for fun (and believe me, I have invested heavily in working that way so it can’t really either), I have decided also to start making music the old LoFi way as a reaction to it. I have began collecting vintage keyboards that I have to play by hand and recording on an old digital multitrack recorder I have had since the early 2000’s. The more human my music is, the less ease it can be replicated by machine with. It maintains scarcity and value that way and I can sell it so I have something I can reinvest in making music still. This has also affected how I use a computer since Microsoft’s roll out of forced AI integration with windows 11. Along with what they’re doing to the operating system which is abominable that you can’t opt out of, they have caused a perfectly good laptop of mine to go obsolete over night. I will still continue running it without the updates but that can’t be forever. In the meantime, I have been using an old Thinkpad T420 with Ubuntu on it so I don’t have to deal with Microsoft’s shit. So tl;dr my new hyperfocuses are cassettes, vintage keyboards, Lofi and 80’s music and Linux computing. Mega post incoming! I have fallen into a bunch of new hyper focuses recently. As a grass-roots media company focused on philanthropy, it's important to us that our content remains free of charge and isn't hidden behind a paywall. summary: 8. doldrums - blubrd.
text: 8. doldrums - blubrd. 8. doldrums - blubrd. This week I decided to review my brother’s new album, both to support him and because I want to spread the word of his great music! As someone who doesn’t really listen to rap, it felt a bit daunting and difficult to write a good review without much background knowledge of the genre. However, I do have some familiarity with the sort of rap that my brother listens to and which I can hear has definitely influenced doldrums, especially in the types of samples and the impressionistic, jazzy sound. The samples are utilised so well in doldrums , feeling unexpected, fun, abstract, and atmospheric all at once. I found it really interesting that many of the samples are so short and repeated in such brief loops, yet they work really effectively in creating a hook and a rhythm in each song. I am also always so impressed by the intelligence of blubrd.’s songwriting, and doldrums is no exception with its great balance of witty references and personal, emotional lyrics. This is the sort of album where you notice new little lyrical details each listen, and pop culture nerds in particular will be rewarded by mentions of iconic film franchises like Star Wars and The Matrix. I liked noticing the little connections between some songs too – “you flew too close to the sun” in ‘pond’, and “feel like Icarus did, got wind in my wings” in ‘delight/outro’ felt like a perfect full circle moment. doldrums is light and deep at the same time – the piano samples in ‘pharaoh’ float dazzlingly and ‘delight/outro’ overflows with ecstasy and confidence, in contrast with the dark, murky beats of tracks like ‘pond’ which feel more representative of the heavy and evocative album title. This album is overall a really effective portrayal of the ups and downs of everyday life and the feeling of trying to stay motivated through it all. With this in mind, ‘delight/outro’ couldn’t be a better choice for the album’s closer track – the song title says it all, this really feels like a moment of hope and life looking up after a bleak period. This album is such a big achievement for blubrd. – I’m so proud of him as my brother and as an artist in his own right, and I’m really excited to see what he does next! Favourite songs: pharaoh, delight/outro, slice of life Favourite moments: The really cleverly-used samples in ‘delight/outro’ – the applause, the whistle, and the scoffing sounds are responsive to the lyrics in a way that is super entertaining, fun, and satisfying. The way there is just one great line after another in every song!! “Only friends I trust are my critics”, “preaching to the choir like crickets” in ‘pharaoh’, “yeah you salty like some mixed nuts” in ‘delight/outro’, I could go on and on listing them all. 8. doldrums - blubrd. summary: Is this what it feels like to be in love? Pray for Rain by Israel's Arcade, live at Club Congress If there's at least one part...
text: Is this what it feels like to be in love? Pray for Rain by Israel's Arcade, live at Club Congress If there's at least one part... Is this what it feels like to be in love? Pray for Rain by Israel's Arcade, live at Club Congress If there's at least one part you need to listen to, the moment I felt this song rip me apart, skip to 2:00-2:47 I went to this concert alone to practice confidence in doing fun things independently, instead of my usual impulse to depend on someone coming with me to a place only I really want to go. I'm glad I did this alone, as I took by myself toward the front of the crowd near the stage, it was only Israel's voice, the band's beautiful music, the glittering lights reflecting off the overheads disco balls, and? Me. I've searched for love so long in another person, I forgot what it was like to fall in love with my own bliss like this. Is this what it feels like to be in love?
